data.title: US Navy restricting press access to sailors' service history
data.description:
The U.S. Navy will no longer release service history information for sailors, ending the decades-long practice to address what officials describe as security threats and harassment concerns.

The move comes amid greater press restrictions imposed by Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, which include a requirement for reporters to be escorted by authorized personnel in the Pentagon. 

Under the new policy, the Navy will typically withhold information such as a sailor’s duty assignments, awards and service history that was traditionally made available to journalists and the public upon request.

Capt. Candice Tresch, a spokesperson for the Chief of Naval Personnel, told The Associated Press that the Navy will continue to confirm whether an individual is a sailor only if the request is deemed of “high public interest.”

The policy follows guidance issued last week by Navy spokesperson Rear Adm. John Robinson, directing the service to remove public-facing information about commanding officers. Tresch told the AP that the policy was broadened to cover all sailors “in the interest of equality.”

When the AP had inquired about a biography of a Blue Angels pilot following a low-level flyover at a Florida beach last week, the outlet reported that the Navy cited the new policy when declining to provide the biography. 

The office refused despite information such as names, photographs and descriptions already appearing on the Blue Angels’ public website. Tresch later provided the biography to the AP after the outlet inquired further.

In a statement to the AP, Tresch said the Pentagon will now only release such details of the sailor if the person is “a public figure or extenuating circumstances exist.”

The new policy marks a broader push by the Pentagon to limit public access to military information. CBS News reported that the Pentagon asked Congress to create a new exemption under the Freedom of Information Act to be able to withhold unclassified access from public access.

data.title: Vance detail Secret Service agent suspected of leaking information put on administrative leave
data.description:
The U.S. Secret Service said Thursday it was investigating a member of Vice President Vance’s detail for allegedly leaking sensitive information.

“A member of the Vice Presidential Protective Division is the subject of an administrative investigation, and potential criminal inquiry, involving allegations of compromising operational and information security,” Secret Service Chief of Communications Anthony Guglielmi said in a statement to The Hill.

Guglielmi said the agency will not comment on any specifics about the case but added that “any conduct that potentially threatens the safety of a protectee will not be tolerated.”

The investigation comes one week after an MS NOW story reported on frustrations among Vance’s security detail over sudden and immediate trips and assignments. This included internal concerns “about Vance and his office pressing them for trips and assignments that some agents consider an inappropriate or even unprecedented use of government resources compared to prior vice presidents.”

The agency told the outlet that previous vice presidents “eschewed using such expensive government perks for the convenience of their children’s schedule, and agents typically drove children locally in sports utility vehicles.”

The story cited multiple sources, including unnamed Trump administration officials with knowledge of Vance’s flight plans, schedule and the agents’ “frustration,” particularly over last-minute travel requests from the vice president and second lady Usha Vance.

Current and former Secret Service personnel told MS NOW that such “off-the-record” trips require agents to cancel their own plans, including taking time off, and race to the location where they are needed while also quickly developing security plans.

“The detail is tired of them not giving notice on things and making everything an OTR,” one source familiar with the detail’s frustration told the outlet. “He [Vance] thinks he can still move around like a U.S. Senator.”

Frustration among the agents has resulted in some making custom coins and stickers mocking the vice president and his family’s irregular trips. MS NOW reviewed images with Vance’s Secret Service code name, “Bobcat,” used on coins reading “Bobcat OTR Survivors Club.”

CNN, which first reported the leaked investigation, reported that the agent placed on administrative leave is suspected of being the source for MS NOW’s story.

Similar leaks over the Qatari-gifted jet for Air Force One and security concerns reported in The New York Times prompted the Department of Justice (DOJ) to subpoena four of the outlet’s reporters.

The DOJ previously told The Hill that the reporters “are not the targets, those leaking classified information are.”

“We recognize there may always be natural tension there, but we are not going to ignore the law and stop investigating the people who work in the administration and think it’s OK to leak classified information impacting national security,” the spokesperson said.

data.title: Warren presses Trump CFPB nominee on conflicts of interest, workforce cuts
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) on Thursday pressed President Trump’s nominee to lead the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) regarding conflicts of interest and the Trump administration’s cuts to the agency. 

During Brian Johnson’s nomination hearing in front of the Senate Banking Committee, ranking member Warren noted the consumer protection watchdog, under acting director Russell Vought, dropped a lawsuit against Capital One last year after the company donated $1 million to Trump’s inaugural committee.

The initial CFPB lawsuit, which the agency filed during the final days of the Biden administration, alleged Capital One cheated consumers out of more than $2 billion in interest payments on their savings accounts. 

Warren asked Johnson, “Will you commit to notifying Congress and the inspector general [of the Federal Reserve] immediately if the White House contacts you about an enforcement action against a Trump family company or a big donor that cheated families?”

Johnson replied that during his two-plus years at the CFPB during the first Trump administration, such a scenario “never happened.” 

The Massachusetts Democrat followed up, “That’s not my question. My question is, if it does, will you notify Congress, and will you notify the inspector general?”

She continued, “Look, there’s obviously a problem here. I’m looking for a way to clean it up,” and repeated her question to Johnson. But the nominee disputed the premise of Warren’s question.

“I don’t care whether you like the premise. I have given you a set of facts that are not made up. It’s happening right now,” Warren remarked, pointing at the dais for emphasis.

“I want your commitment that if it happens on your watch, you’ll pick up the phone and call us,” the progressive lawmaker added, gesturing to her fellow senators on the panel. “Our job is oversight. That’s not so hard. Are you willing to do that?”

Johnson then said he was “not going to engage in a hypothetical,” but he could not finish his sentence before Warren cut him off. 

“Ok, so the answer is no. You will not try to hose out the corruption,” Warren said. 

Since November 2024, Johnson has worked as Capital One’s vice president of U.S. card compliance, according to his LinkedIn. While Trump nominated him to serve as director of the CFPB last month, Johnson acknowledged to Warren he still works at the bank. 

When the Massachusetts Democrat said Capital One is paying Johnson as he is testifying, the nominee said he was appearing before the panel in his “personal” capacity. 

“Okay,” Warren replied, chuckling. “But they’re paying you to get confirmed for this job. I understand they’re also letting you keep a bonus that you have earned when you leave.

“You know, they must be really excited about getting you confirmed here.”

Johnson signed an agreement earlier this month pledging to recuse himself from any matters involving Capital One for two years if he is confirmed, Banking Dive reported on Tuesday.

Multiple Democratic senators, including Warren, also questioned Johnson about the cuts Vought has proposed to the CFPB’s workforce during his 17-plus months in charge.

A federal appeals court last month blocked the Trump administration from immediately firing bureau employees, but a federal judge on July 10 paused the union lawsuit seeking to block the administration from shutting down the watchdog.

The ruling from U.S. District Judge Amy Bernan Jackson, an appointee of former President Obama, allows for Johnson to decide how to move forward regarding cuts if the Senate confirms him. 

When Democratic Sen. Chris Van Hollen (Md.) asked Johnson whether he, like Vought, believes the administration should “eliminate” the CFPB, the nominee said that is not his “intention.”

“The CFPB is a creature of statute. Congress has assigned to it important law to implement and execute, and my intention is to execute the law,” Johnson said.

But when Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) asked Johnson near the end of the hearing whether Vought has done anything he disagrees with, the nominee said, “None come to mind at the moment.”

data.title: O'Reilly: Trump should 'take the next month off'
data.description:
Political commentator Bill O’Reilly on Wednesday said President Trump should “take the next month off” as the 80-year-old chief executive faces widening foreign and domestic dilemmas.

O’Reilly, 76, wrapped up the latest episode of his podcast, “No Spin News,” with a reflection on the roughly 40 years that he has known the president. He said he has noticed Trump “hunching over a little bit and that’s normal,” before prescribing his recommendation to the president.

“So my advice to Donald Trump is to take the next month off if you can get into an environment where you like golf and relax a little bit,” O’Reilly said. “You got to deal with Iran, I understand that. Everybody understands it. But you can do that on the phone and you can do that with your people coming.”

The former Fox News host added that Trump needs “a breather.”

“I need a breather and I’m going to build it into August. But he needs a breather much more than I do,” O’Reilly said, adding that he wishes the best for Trump and all of his predecessors.

The remarks come as Trump sees conflict in the Middle East widening with the Houthis striking two Saudi Arabian oil tankers in the Red Sea, an alternate passage for oil exports after Iran shut down the Strait of Hormuz in response to the resumption of hostilities between the Islamic regime and the U.S.

These closures have impacted the path of transit for oil exports, causing energy prices to soar with the U.S. national gas average reaching $4.09 on Thursday, according to AAA.

Trump’s health has also been in the spotlight since before his return to the White House last year. Before turning 80 last month, bruising on his hands and swelling of his ankles raised questions about his physical fitness. Questions were also raised by critics following several instances of the president closing his eyes during events in the Oval Office.

White House physician Sean Barbabella said in July 2024 that the president was diagnosed with chronic venous insufficiency, noting that the bruising was “consistent with minor soft tissue damage from frequent handshaking.”

Trump himself also criticized and mocked former President Biden’s health, fixating on his use of an autopen to raise questions about Biden’s cognitive abilities while in office. Biden, at the time, was the oldest man inaugurated at 78 years and 61 days old before Trump returned to the White House at 78 and 220 days old.

data.title: Watch live: Trump gives remarks on expanding Ratepayer Protection Pledge
data.description:
President Trump will deliver remarks Thursday afternoon on the White House’s plan to expand a voluntary ratepayer protection pledge aimed at shielding consumers from electricity cost increases tied to AI data center growth.

The announcement comes as the Ratepayer Protection Act is gaining steam in the House. The legislation, if passed, would require states to “consider” standards that place the costs associated with the sprawling AI infrastructure on tech companies rather than individuals.

Earlier this year, the Trump administration said it was seeking agreements with Big Tech companies to pay for the increased power costs in areas where they build new data centers.

The event at the Environmental Protection Agency headquarters in Washington is scheduled to begin at 2:30 p.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: 'Culture of creepiness': Republican says she wouldn't want daughter on Capitol Hill
data.description:
Rep. Kat Cammack (R-Fla.) said Wednesday that Congress had a “culture of creepiness” when it came to members approaching and soliciting staffers.

Cammack told MeidasTouch’s Pablo Manríquez that she “wouldn’t feel comfortable sending my daughter to work here” in the current congressional environment. 

Her comments came after Rep. Jeff Crank (R-Colo.) unveiled a resolution that would mandate lawmakers to confidentially disclose relationships with staffers in other offices or committees to the House Committee on Ethics.

“I think we would be better off if members were more focused on their work than enhancing their love life,” Cammack said.

She commended the resolution in addressing the power dynamics of these situations.

“People don’t recognize that even if it’s another staffer in another office, you still have tremendous influence over that congressional staffer’s career,” she said.

The House in June adopted a resolution from Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) concerning sexual harassment violations among members and staffers. 

The resolution directed the Ethics Committee and the Office of Congressional Workplace Rights (OCWR) to produce a “single consolidated list” that includes the name of each member who was the subject of a sexual harassment investigation in violation of House rules that resulted in a monetary settlement. 

But the OCWR stated in July that, per the Congressional Accountability Act in 2018, it had not received notification about settlements relating to sexual harassment, sexual misconduct or sexual abuse by a member. 

Cammack emphasized in her comments that sexual misconduct in Congress is a “really big effing problem.”

Former Reps. Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) and Tony Gonzales (R-Texas) both resigned in response to sexual assault and harassment allegations from former staffers. Swalwell dropped out of the California governor’s race amid accusations by a former staffer of sexual assault but has denied wrongdoing. Gonzales admitted to having an affair with a staffer who later committed suicide. 

The House Ethics Committee revealed in April that it had conducted 20 sexual misconduct investigations into lawmakers since 2017, with 5 cases remaining confidential.

data.title: Cassidy will support CDC nominee, but attendance issues force vote postponement
data.description:
Erica Schwartz, President Trump’s pick to lead the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), picked up a key endorsement from Senate Health Committee Chair Bill Cassidy (R-La.) Thursday morning, but a scheduled vote on her nomination was postponed. 

“I’m confident that she knows what she is doing and will stand against those who do not,” Cassidy said when the committee convened.  

During her confirmation hearing last week, Cassidy questioned Schwartz about whether she could protect the agency from political meddling and stand up to Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. if he asked her to take action that was not supported by scientific evidence. 

Her predecessor, Susan Monarez, pushed back against Kennedy’s vaccine agenda and was fired after being on the job less than a month. 

Cassidy noted that “as a sign of good faith,” the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) had modified the CDC’s website on vaccines and autism, which Kennedy ordered to be changed last year to include a disclaimer that vaccines do not cause autism “is not an evidence-based claim.” 

“There’s more to do to repair the damage that has been done to the U.S. public health response, but this is a start,” Cassidy said. 

The Republican senator, who voted to confirm Kennedy despite misgivings, has since become one of the secretary’s main GOP critics after losing his primary election to Rep. Julia Letlow (R-La.), who was backed by Trump. 

Cassidy seemed unsatisfied with Schwartz’s answers last week and it wasn’t immediately clear if he would support her nomination. His support makes it likely Schwartz will advance to the full Senate.  

However, that will need to wait until next week as the vote Thursday was postponed due to attendance issues.  

The panel’s minority leader Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), said that while he was “impressed” with Schwartz’s career as a public health civil servant, he did not share Cassidy’s optimism about her ability to stand up to Kennedy and would vote against her. 

“Will we have people in the Trump administration who have the guts to stand up to people who are anti-science and into conspiracy theories? And I am afraid that right now, within HHS, we have a secretary who will not tolerate this,” Sanders said.  

“I will be voting against Dr. Schwartz because we need people in this country who are going to stand up, protect the American people, and I don’t think you can see that right now with Secretary Kennedy in his position,” Sanders added.  

The committee earlier Thursday delayed a vote on a Trump administration nominee to lead an agency focused on public health preparedness due to a lack of support. 

Sean Kaufman, nominated to lead the Administration for Strategic Preparedness and Response, was confronted by tough questions during a confirmation hearing last week over his views on vaccines. 

“There are ongoing conversations with the administration on the path forward for Mr. Kaufman’s nomination,” a spokesperson for the committee said in a statement. 

In a series of posts over the past several years, Kaufman argued against giving the hepatitis B vaccine at birth and cited the disproven link between vaccines and autism. 

During the hearing, Kaufman admitted to having authored a LinkedIn post saying he would “rather perish” than give his children the COVID-19 vaccine. 

Kaufman sought to distance himself from those past comments during the hearing and insisted he believed “vaccines save lives.” But he waffled when pressed by a number of senators about whether he supported HHS’s decision to defund mRNA vaccine research and said it was important to study the risks and benefits of the COVID-19 vaccine, citing some rare adverse events.

data.title: Thune responds to Leavitt quip that Trump's patience with him 'running out' over SAVE America Act
data.description:
Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) bristled when asked Thursday about White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt’s statement that President Trump’s “patience is running out” with the Senate GOP leader because of the stalled SAVE America Act.

“Maybe she or somebody else ought to get on the phone and get the votes. Right? It’s 50 around here,” Thune shot back, when asked about Leavitt’s statement to reporters at a press briefing earlier Thursday.

“Instead of pointing the finger at Republicans, they might think about going after the people who are stopping it on the floor, which is the Democrats,” the senator added, appearing somewhat irritated by the critical comment. “And if there are Republicans they think are gettable, get on the phone.”

Leavitt said Trump’s patience with Thune’s handling of the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) America Act, which the Senate has voted on — in whole or in part — at least five times but has failed to move forward, is wearing thin.

The remark comes as the president has ramped up his push for Republicans to support the voter ID bill, legislation that would require proof of citizenship for voter registration and photo ID’s when casting ballots.

“The president spoke about this directly yesterday,” she told reporters. “His patience is running out.

“He wants to see as much of the SAVE America passed as possible by the August recess. He knows that’s what the American people want to see passed. He was just traveling on the road yesterday, spoke about this and there was a very loud applause from the people in the audience, the everyday Americans because voter ID is common sense,” Leavitt continued. “They want proof of citizenship in our elections and the president feels very strongly about that.”

“The House did its job, the Senate needs to do its job too,” she added.

Trump complained about the lack of action in the Senate during a trip to Georgia Wednesday and suggested he is holding back on his criticism of Senate Republicans.

“The Senate is a place you send things when you want them to die,” the president grumbled. “We’re not going to take this any longer, we’re not going to take it. I’m trying to be nice.”

The House on Wednesday passed a budget framework that would set the stage for passing a $95 billion reconciliation package, including $10 billion for election-related reforms, but the measure has an uncertain future in the Senate.

Thune on Thursday said he doesn’t have 50 GOP votes right now to advance the House-passed budget measure.

“As I’ve said before you got to get to 50 and I can’t count to 50 right now on a budget resolution,” the Republican leader said, when asked about the House-passed budget resolution.

“We’ll see. We’ll have those conversations with the conference. The priority is funding the government. To me that’s the thing we got to keep our eyes on,” he added.

Thune also told reporters earlier in the week that he would “hold” a House-passed budget resolution in the Senate to use as a possible vehicle to pass a short-term government funding resolution in September.

data.title: Mark Sanford announces run for Lindsey Graham's Senate seat
data.description:
Former Rep. Mark Sanford (R-S.C.) is making a run for the late Sen. Lindsey Graham’s (R-S.C.) seat after his death, Sanford announced Thursday morning.

“Our country is at a financial tipping point, and I can’t sit on the sidelines while Washington adds a trillion dollars to the debt every 100 days,” Sanford said in a post on the social platform X Thursday.

“I’ve spent my entire time in politics fighting this fight, and I’m running for U.S. Senate to keep the American dream alive for our sons, my grandson, and those you love. Join us,” he added.

Sanford’s post also featured a video of himself in which he said Graham’s “death is a reminder to all of us of how short and precious life is.”

“My own dad got Lou Gehrig’s disease when I was in high school, and this notion of all of us having just a little time has always hit me hard. We’re stewards of our lives and the things that we love for just a short time,” he added.

Sanford served South Carolina’s 1st Congressional District between 1995 and 2001, and then again from 2013 to 2019. He was also the Palmetto State’s governor from 2003 to 2011. 

In April, Sanford ended a campaign for his old House seat after only about a month. He had said in a press release that rather than continue with the campaign, he would start a national nonprofit and grassroots movement centered around tackling the national debt.

The former South Carolina governor has a history of criticizing President Trump, from frequently criticizing Trump in his first term, which led to his defeat in a GOP primary in 2018, to mounting a brief challenge to Trump in the Republican presidential primary in 2020.

Sen. Darline Graham (R-S.C.), the late Graham’s sister, is also seeking a full six-year term in her brother’s old seat alongside Reps. Russell Fry (R-S.C.) and Ralph Norman (R-S.C.), with a primary for the GOP nominee on Aug. 11.

data.title: Live updates: House OKs, Senate rejects war powers resolution; Leavitt reiterates Saudi deal's Abraham Accords condition
data.description:
President Trump is threatening to intensify the war with Iran as some Republican lawmakers urge him to “finish the job,” but there is also widespread skepticism on Capitol Hill on both sides of the aisle about whether he can deliver a victory.

The House narrowly approved a Democratic-sponsored war powers resolution with the help of four GOP lawmakers. The Senate will vote on a separate but similar resolution shortly. They come as Trump’s support for the Iran conflict is reaching a new low among his base.

The Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen on Thursday attacked two oil tankers in the Red Sea, a ramping up of the conflict since the group announced a blockade of Saudi ships attempting to go through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait earlier this week. 

Dr. Erica Schwartz faced a vote in the Senate Health Committee for her nomination to be director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, but it was postponed due to attendance.

The Energy Department announced Wednesday a nuclear cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia, and it drew swift backlash. On Thursday morning, Trump added a condition: The Saudis must join the Abraham Accords. Insiders can read more here.

On Thursday afternoon, Trump is set to visit the Environmental Protection Agency and will make a statement about the Ratepayer Protection Pledge that he issued in March in which U.S. tech companies cover the electricity costs for their data centers, not consumers.

Follow live below.
